title: Gulyásleves
subtitle: Hungarian goulash soup
excerpt: Beef, potato, and pepper simmered in a deeply paprika-spiced broth. Gulyásleves is the soup form of goulash — leaner and brothier than the stew, but just as red, just as warming, and in some ways more satisfying.
sourceNotes: Gulyásleves (goulash soup) is the original form of gulyás — the word gulyás referred to a herdsman's stew cooked on the Hungarian plains, and the soup version was the everyday preparation, eaten directly from the cauldron. The thicker stew form (pörkölt) came later. The soup uses the same paprika-and-beef base but more liquid, with potato added to give body. It is one of the most widely eaten soups in Hungary and one of the few Hungarian dishes internationally recognisable by name.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the potato — it becomes grainy on thawing. Reheat the soup base and add freshly diced potato, simmering until tender.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Make the base in bulk and freeze in portions without the potato.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The flavour improves the next day. Make up to 2 days ahead, refrigerating without the potato, and add freshly cooked potato when reheating.

content.text: The rule for paprika applies here as it does in halászlé: remove the pan from the heat before adding the paprika, then immediately add liquid to prevent it burning. Burned paprika is bitter and ruins the soup. The paprika should go in when the fat is fragrant with cooked onion, and the stock should follow within seconds.
The beef needs time. Cut it into 2 cm pieces and do not rush the simmering — it needs 60 to 70 minutes to become completely tender. The potato goes in for the last 20 minutes only.

items.prepNote: peeled and diced into 1.5 cm cubes

content.text: Warm the {{sunflower-oil}} in a large saucepan over medium-high heat. Brown the {{beef-shin}} in batches until coloured on all sides, 4 to 5 minutes per batch. Remove and set aside. In the same pan, cook the {{onion}} over medium heat for 8 minutes until soft and golden. Add the {{garlic}} and {{caraway-seeds}} and cook for 1 minute. Remove from the heat and immediately stir in the {{paprika-sweet}}. Return to the heat and add 1.5 litres of water. Add the browned beef, {{pepper-green}}, {{tomato}}, and {{bay-leaves}}. Bring to a simmer.

Simmer partially covered for 60 to 70 minutes until the beef is completely tender. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Add the {{potato}} and simmer for a further 20 minutes until the potato is cooked through. Remove the bay leaves. The soup should be a rich red-orange with clearly defined pieces of beef and potato. Serve with bread.

items.cause: Not enough time, or cut too large
items.fix: Continue simmering — shin can take 90 minutes to become completely tender. Do not rush. Top up with water if the level drops

items.cause: Too little paprika, or paprika burned before liquid was added
items.fix: Stir in an extra tablespoon of sweet paprika mixed with a little water to a paste, and simmer for 10 minutes. The paste method avoids burning

items.cause: Added too early or simmered too long
items.fix: Add the potato only in the last 20 minutes. Waxy potato holds up better than floury; do not stir more than necessary once it is in the pot

content.text: Gulyásleves is Hungarian peasant food in the most direct sense: a meal made by herdsmen from the cattle they drove across the puszta, cooked in a cauldron over an open fire, eaten with bread. The modern version is a domestic soup, made in a saucepan on a kitchen hob, but the principle is the same: beef, paprika, and time. It is the most internationally recognisable Hungarian dish, served in Hungarian restaurants from Budapest to Buenos Aires, and it is made better at home than in almost any of them.
